FULL CONTENT:
# Adaptive Control Strategy for Robot Motion Planning in Complex Environments

## Authors

## DOI:

## Keywords:

## Abstract

Adaptive control strategies play a crucial role in robot motion planning in complex environments, enabling robots to dynamically adjust their behavior based on environmental changes and task requirements. This article reviews various adaptive control methods and their applications in robot motion planning. These methods improve the autonomy, robustness, and safety of robots through real-time perception of the environment and optimization of path planning. This article aims to review the application of adaptive control strategies in robot motion planning in complex environments, explore the collaborative work of various methods and technologies to achieve efficient and reliable robot motion planning, and provide useful references and guidance for the future development of robot technology through the research in this article.

--- SOURCE 5: Adaptive Control Strategy for Quadruped Robots in ... ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2312.17606v1

SUMMARY:
This framework produces a unified control strategy, enabling the robot to sustain its locomotion and perform tasks despite sudden joint actuator

FULL CONTENT:
# Adaptive Control Strategy for Quadruped Robots in Actuator Degradation Scenarios

###### Abstract.

Quadruped robots have strong adaptability to extreme environments but may also experience faults.
Once these faults occur, robots must be repaired before returning to the task, reducing their practical feasibility.
One prevalent concern among these faults is actuator degradation, stemming from factors like device aging or unexpected operational events.
Traditionally, addressing this problem has relied heavily on intricate fault-tolerant design, which demands deep domain expertise from developers and lacks generalizability.
Learning-based approaches offer effective ways to mitigate these limitations, but a research gap exists in effectively deploying such methods on real-world quadruped robots.
This paper introduces a pioneering teacher-student framework rooted in reinforcement learning, named Actuator Degradation Adaptation Transformer (Adapt), aimed at addressing this research gap.
This framework produces a unified control strategy, enabling the robot to sustain its locomotion and perform tasks despite sudden joint actuator faults, relying exclusively on its internal sensors.
Empirical evaluations on the Unitree A1 platform validate the deployability and effectiveness of Adapt on real-world quadruped robots, and affirm the robustness and practicality of our approach.

![Refer to caption](x1.png)

## 1. Introduction

Legged robots, known for their multifunctionality, have demonstrated remarkable adaptability and flexibility in traversing complex terrains and navigating diverse and unfamiliar environments (Miki etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib28); Bellicoso etВ al., [2018](#bib.bib4)).
As hardware technology and control algorithms advance, quadruped robots have evolved to possess improved load-bearing capacities, heightened stability, and structural advantages (Biswal and Mohanty, [2021](#bib.bib6)).
These advancements have resulted in their growing deployment across diverse domains such as exploration (Miki etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib28)), search and rescue (Krotkov etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib21)), military applications (Kemper and Kolain, [2022](#bib.bib18)), and industrial scenarios (Bellicoso etВ al., [2018](#bib.bib4)).

However, in real-world applications, the robustness of quadruped robots is critically compromised by their susceptibility to various fault scenarios.
While these robots excel in navigating complex and unpredictable environments (Biswal and Mohanty, [2021](#bib.bib6)), their adaptability renders them vulnerable to various fault scenarios, such as physical damage, joint malfunctions, and motor aging.
Such vulnerabilities can have far-reaching implications, leading to actuator degradation and consequent reductions in operational efficiency (Liu etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib25); Khalastchi and Kalech, [2018](#bib.bib19)).
Moreover, these faults not only inconvenience operators but, in extreme cases, may also jeopardize the longevity of the robot and pose significant safety risks to humans.
Therefore, how to deal with such a fault has drawn significant attention from researchers.

Dealing with the fault in real-time is exceptionally challenging due to obstacles like distance-related accessibility issues and hazardous conditions.
Additionally, immediate fault rectification often necessitates specialized expertise and tools, which may not be readily available.
As a result, the design of fault-tolerant control algorithms becomes crucial, empowering robots to adapt to varying degrees of malfunction autonomously (Cui etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib12); Chen etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib11)).
Implementing such algorithms not only prolongs the robotвҖҷs operational lifespan but also substantially enhances its reliability. This, in turn, broadens the range of real-world applications where quadruped robots can be effectively deployed.

While traditional fault-tolerant algorithms like Model Predictive Control (MPC) and whole-body control frameworks have been widely adopted, they suffer from concrete drawbacks such as heavy reliance on domain expertise, labor-intensive manual tuning, and limited adaptability to unforeseen environmental and mechanical conditions (DiВ Carlo etВ al., [2018](#bib.bib13); Khalastchi and Kalech, [2018](#bib.bib19); Cui etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib12); Liu etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib25)).
These conventional approaches focus on the active detection and confirmation of faults during the robotвҖҷs operation. If a fault is identified, a pre-engineered alternative control scheme is invoked to mitigate the issue.
However, the effectiveness of these strategies is intrinsically tied to the designerвҖҷs comprehensive understanding of the robotвҖҷs mechanical intricacies, often necessitating meticulous manual adjustments (Guo etВ al., [2019](#bib.bib16)).
Moreover, the rigidity of relying on predefined alternative control schemes becomes a significant constraint in real-world applications.
Specifically, these methods struggle to adapt to novel environmental or mechanical scenarios, frequently requiring the formulation of separate, customized control strategies for different variants of the same fault (Liu etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib25)).

While learning-based approaches, particularly DRL, offer substantial advantages such as autonomous skill acquisition and reduced dependency on domain expertise, they also present specific challenges, including sample efficiency, algorithmic stability, and the complexity of bridging the simulation-to-reality gap (Margolis etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib27); Arulkumaran etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib3); Lai etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib23)).

This paradigm empowers robots to acquire emergency-response skills independently (Guo etВ al., [2019](#bib.bib16)), reducing the dependence on domain knowledge when designing algorithms to some extent (Liu etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib25); Allard etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib2)).
Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has gained prominence in quadruped robot control due to its ability to learn nonlinear control strategies (Margolis etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib27)).
Unlike traditional control methods, DRL does not require precise models and effectively manages high-dimensional state spaces (Arulkumaran etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib3)).
To balance exploration and exploitation, reinforcement learning algorithms consistently develop strategies with enhanced adaptability and generalization capabilities (Lai etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib23); Margolis etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib27); Wu etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib44); Yu etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib46)).
Furthermore, the availability of physically accurate modeling simulation environments like IsaacGym (Makoviychuk etВ al., [2021](#bib.bib26)) and massively parallel solutions (Rudin etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib37)) has significantly elevated the efficiency of control strategy learning while easing the transfer from simulation to real-world environments.

While substantial progress has been made in enhancing robot motion performance and enabling cross-terrain capabilities under stable operational conditions (Kumar etВ al., [2021](#bib.bib22); Lee etВ al., [2020](#bib.bib24)), there remains a notable gap in exploring actuator degradation fault within the context of DRL for real-world quadruped robots.

In response to bridge the gap,
we present a pioneering teacher-student training framework that combines transformer-based architectures with reinforcement learning principles,
named Actuator Degeneration Adaptation Transformer (Adapt).
This framework equips the quadruped robot to adapt to joint actuator degradation faults within predetermined thresholds, relying solely on its perceptual abilities. Our proposed framework demonstrates remarkable robustness within simulated environments and showcases the potential for zero-shot transfer to real-world robots.
This learning-based strategy reduces the dependence on deep expertise in the field of robotics or specific robot models while also holding significant potential for acquiring a broader range of skills.

This paper contributes three key aspects to quadruped robot control amidst joint actuator degradation faults.

Firstly, we tackle an issue of varying degrees of actuator degradation in an intricate simulation environment. We approach this as a type of cross-embodiment task using reinforcement learning techniques that can handle various actuator degradation scenarios instead of certain pre-defined cases.

Secondly, we introduce Adapt, a novel adaptation framework that demonstrates significant generalization potential in experiments.

Lastly, we successfully deployed Adapt on real-world quadruped robots and impressively demonstrated the capability for zero-shot transferring from simulation to real-world robots.

These contributions collectively pave the way for more resilient and versatile quadruped robot locomotion strategies.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to successfully deploy a learning-based approach in addressing actuator degradation on real-world quadruped robots.

## 2. RELATED WORK

### 2.1. DRL for Robot Control

Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) holds the potential to reduce the dependence on expertise in robot control by training a policy in simulation and then transferring it to real world. However, itвҖҷs challenging to directly apply the DRL policy to real world due to the discrepancy between simulation and real world, also known as *reality gap* (Koos etВ al., [2010](#bib.bib20); Boeing and BrГӨunl, [2012](#bib.bib7)). A lot of works were devoted to bridge the reality gap. For example, Domain Randomization (DR) (Tobin etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib41); Peng etВ al., [2018](#bib.bib31)) proposes to train policy in a wide range of environments with various parameters and noises, which makes policy more robust. Additionally, one can also adapt the policy trained in simulator to real-world data in latent space, called domain adaptation (Tan etВ al., [2018](#bib.bib40); FAN etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib15)). Besides, system identification, which tries to identify the physical parameters of real-world explicit or implicit (Yu etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib47); Peng etВ al., [2020](#bib.bib32)), may also help improving the transferring performance.

By leveraging these advanced skills, DRL has achieved remarkable strides in the fields of
legged robot locomotion (Kumar etВ al., [2021](#bib.bib22); Peng etВ al., [2020](#bib.bib32); Lai etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib23)),
robotic arm manipulation (SengaduВ Suresh etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib39); Bettini etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib5); Romero etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib36)),
and wheels robot application (Zhao etВ al., [2023a](#bib.bib49); Zhang, [2023](#bib.bib48); Chen etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib9)).
Within the domain of quadruped robots,
Lee et al. (Lee etВ al., [2020](#bib.bib24)) and Kumar et al. (Kumar etВ al., [2021](#bib.bib22)) developed the teacher-student training paradigm for quadruped robot learning, which has been demonstrated to be highly effective in experiments.
Lai et al. (Lai etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib23)) introduced a two-stage training framework called TERT, demonstrating its superior performance across diverse, challenging terrains, showcasing robust task mobility.
Nahrendra et al. (Nahrendra etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib29)) presented a robust framework for quadrupedal locomotion, enabling stable movement across unstructured terrains.
Peng et al. (Peng etВ al., [2020](#bib.bib32)) presented a robust framework allowing legged robots to acquire agile locomotion skills by imitating real-world animalsвҖҷ movements.
Escontrela et al. (Escontrela etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib14)) presented an effective style reward introducing Adversarial Motion Prior approach into quadrupted robot control.
Similarly, Wu et al. (Wu etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib44)) formulated a single policy, trained through DRL, to achieve a harmonious balance between robust and agile quadrupedal locomotion.
Meanwhile, Yu et al. (Yu etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib46)) developed a learning-based control method to control quadrupedal robots with varying morphologies effectively.

Notably, the works mentioned above primarily revolve around the premise of the robotвҖҷs optimal operation, with seldom specific provisions for fault tolerance in the presence of malfunctions.

### 2.2. Fault-Tolerant Quadrupedal Locomotion

Common robot faults can be categorized into вҖқlockedвҖқ and вҖқuncontrollableвҖқ (Chen etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib11)). In the former, the malfunctioning joint becomes immovable and can be used to provide support, while in the latter, the joint becomes freely movable and loses its supporting function.
This paper mainly focuses on the вҖқuncontrollableвҖқ fault scenario, which presents higher challenges and lacks a relevant learning-based framework for quadruped robots.

Numerous studies have delved into alternative strategies, including traditional control theories and other methodologies, to tackle the challenge of fault tolerance in quadruped robots.
For instance, Cui et al. (Cui etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib12)) and Chen et al. (Chen etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib11)) introduced control-based methods to enable quadruped robots to overcome single-joint locking scenarios.
Zhao et al. (Zhao etВ al., [2023b](#bib.bib50)) devised an adaptive fault-tolerant control law to address leg joint actuator faults in quadruped robots.
Allard et al. (Allard etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib2)) and their team have undertaken a series of endeavors rooted in Quality-Diversity that successfully tackled the challenge of damage recovery in real-world hexapod robots.
This approach, inherent in autonomous learning, showcases significant potential.

However, a common challenge these methods share is their substantial reliance on task-specific expertise, as they exhibit a heightened dependence on the specification of lower-level behavioral logic. In contrast, the end-to-end learning capabilities of deep reinforcement learning, coupled with the inherent adaptive autonomy, can yield a robust and stable control strategy to address robot faults.
Okamoto et al. (Okamoto and Kawamoto, [2020](#bib.bib30)) demonstrated the feasibility of deep reinforcement learning in tackling actuator failure within the ant-v2 environment of the OpenAI Gym (Brockman etВ al., [2016](#bib.bib8)).
Yan et al. (Yan etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib45)) introduced a fault-tolerant reinforcement learning control framework for robotic manipulator joint actuator faults.
Furthermore, the DRL-based hardware fault-tolerant controller devised by Wu et al. (Wu etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib44)) for quadruped robot locomotion successfully validates in both simulator and real-world environments. Importantly, their emphasis was on the вҖқlockedвҖқ fault scenario, as previously highlighted, which is notably distinct from the central focus of our investigation.

## 3. PRELIMINARIES

### 3.2. Adversarial Motion Prior

The design of reward functions for quadrupedal locomotion is non-trivial and usually needs much domain knowledge and human effort (Kumar etВ al., [2021](#bib.bib22); Lee etВ al., [2020](#bib.bib24)).
To address this issue, Adversarial Motion Prior (AMP) (Escontrela etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib14)) is proposed to simplify the reward function design by utilizing some pre-collected expert motions as prior. Specifically, AMP utilizes a similar framework with generative adversarial imitation learning (Gail) (Ho and Ermon, [2016](#bib.bib17)) to discriminate whether a transition (st,st+1)subscriptрқ‘ рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘ рқ‘Ў1(s\_{t},s\_{t+1})( italic\_s start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_t end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_s start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_t + 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is sampled from expert motions. Intuitively, The style function regulates agentsвҖҷ actions with a similar style of reference, which guides the robot into a natural gait.

### 3.3. Decision Transformer

The Transformer architecture (Vaswani etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib42)) has garnered significant attention in various domains, including computer vision, natural language processing, and beyond. The attention mechanisms capture the long-range dependencies and relevance, effectively tackling sequential decision-making problems (WEN etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib43)).
Recognizing its potential, Chen et al. abstracted the reinforcement learning (RL) problem into the framework of conditional sequence modeling, subsequently proposing a transformer-based solution known as Decision Transformer (DT) (Chen etВ al., [2021](#bib.bib10)). By leveraging the autoregressive model, DT predicts actions based on past state-action sequences and expected returns.

Consistent with prior research, we employ the GPT architecture (Radford etВ al., [2018](#bib.bib33)), comprising several stacked self-attention layers with residual connections and causal masking.
The input sequences are embedded as tokens containing position encoding.
Subsequently, each token of this sequence is mapped to key, query, and value pairs kisubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘–k\_{i}italic\_k start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT, qisubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–q\_{i}italic\_q start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and visubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘–v\_{i}italic\_v start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT through the self-attention layers.
The resultant output of the self-attention layer is represented as follows:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| (4) |  | zi=вҲ‘j=1nsoftmax({вҹЁqi,kjвҖІ}jвҖІ=1n)jвӢ…vj.z\_{i}=\sum\_{j=1}^{n}\mathrm{softmax}(\{\langle q\_{i},k\_{j^{\prime}}\}\_{j^{% \prime}=1}^{n})\_{j}\cdot v\_{j}.italic\_z start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = вҲ‘ start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j = 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman\_softmax ( { вҹЁ italic\_q start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_k start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT вҖІ end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT } start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT вҖІ end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT вӢ… italic\_v start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT . |  |

In addition, we use position encoding (Vaswani etВ al., [2017](#bib.bib42)) to model the sequential relevance:

|  |  |  |  |

## 4. Methodology

![Refer to caption](x2.png)

This section comprehensively explains our novel approach, the Actuator Degradation Adaptation Transformer (Adapt).
Adapt is devised as a teacher-student framework meticulously designed to incorporate and leverage actuator degradation information.
The teacher policy within the Adapt framework employs actuator degradation information as prior knowledge.
This utilization of prior information serves the purpose of narrowing down the potential state space during policy exploration.
On the other hand, the student policy within the Adapt framework utilizes actuator degradation information to enhance decision-making processes.

### 4.1. RL-based Control Architecture

The environment is defined precisely as the Markov Decision Process (MDP) outlined in Section [3.1](#S3.SS1 "3.1. Reinforcement Learning for Robotic Locomotion вҖЈ 3. PRELIMINARIES вҖЈ Adaptive Control Strategy for Quadruped Robots in Actuator Degradation Scenarios").

State.
In the context of this article, our data originates solely from the robotвҖҷs internal sensory inputs, without incorporating any external sources or additional information such as images or GPS data.
Consistent with previous designs (Rudin etВ al., [2022](#bib.bib37); Lai etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib23); Yu etВ al., [2023](#bib.bib46)), we use a 48-dimensional vector sвҲҲрқ’®рқ‘ рқ’®s\in\mathcal{S}italic\_s вҲҲ caligraphic\_S as the state. This state vector contains both the base and each jointвҖҷs information (12 joints in total).

The base information includes several key variables:

Base linear velocity vвҲҲв„қ3рқ‘Јsuperscriptв„қ3v\in\mathbb{R}^{3}italic\_v вҲҲ blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. Estimated through comprehensive readings from the IMU accelerometer, the current orientation of the base returned by IMU, and the velocity estimation of the contact leg.

Base angular velocity ПүвҲҲв„қ3рқң”superscriptв„қ3\omega\in\mathbb{R}^{3}italic\_Пү вҲҲ blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. Derived from the IMU gyroscope.

The bodyвҖҷs orientation, represented by the gravity component gвҲҲв„қ3рқ‘”superscriptв„қ3g\in\mathbb{R}^{3}italic\_g вҲҲ blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, is calculated based on the base direction measured by the IMU.

Target linear velocity commands (v^x,v^y)вҲҲв„қ2subscript^рқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙsubscript^рқ‘Јрқ‘Ұsuperscriptв„қ2(\hat{v}\_{x},\hat{v}\_{y})\in\mathbb{R}^{2}( over^ start\_ARG italic\_v end\_ARG start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_x end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , over^ start\_ARG italic\_v end\_ARG start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_y end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) вҲҲ blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and target angular velocity Пү^zвҲҲв„қsubscript^рқң”рқ‘§в„қ\hat{\omega}\_{z}\in\mathbb{R}over^ start\_ARG italic\_Пү end\_ARG start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_z end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT вҲҲ blackboard\_R.

The information for each joint encompasses:

Positions qвҲҲв„қ12рқ‘һsuperscriptв„қ12q\in\mathbb{R}^{12}italic\_q вҲҲ blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 12 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and velocities qЛҷвҲҲв„қ12Лҷрқ‘һsuperscriptв„қ12\dot{q}\in\mathbb{R}^{12}overЛҷ start\_ARG italic\_q end\_ARG вҲҲ blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 12 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT can be retrieved through the motor encoders.

The action aвҖІвҲҲрқ’ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҺвҖІрқ’ңa^{\prime}\in\mathcal{A}italic\_a start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT вҖІ end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT вҲҲ caligraphic\_A from the previous time step will also be considered.

Action.
aвҲҲрқ’ңрқ‘Һрқ’ңa\in\mathcal{A}italic\_a вҲҲ caligraphic\_A, a 12-dimensional vector, signifies the target position of each joint.
Based on insights from traditional control theory, position control is considered safer and more stable than direct torque control.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Future research will focus on developing adaptive control strategies that enhance robots' stability and responsiveness in dynamic environments, enabling seamless adjustments to unforeseen operational challenges.

enriched_points.point_title: Integration of AI and Machine Learning
enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrating AI and machine learning into robotic systems promises to improve decision-making processes, allowing robots to learn from their environments and optimize their performance autonomously.
